title: Stress-test API endpoints under load and capture p50/p95 numbers
description:
# Capture k6 / autocannon baselines for the documented API endpoints

  ## What & Why
  Task #726 shipped the budgets and the index audit, and `docs/performance.md` documents *how* to reproduce a load-test baseline (autocannon recipes are listed). What it does not yet contain is the actual numbers — running the load test requires a long-lived seeded database and the api-server up under realistic concurrency, which the audit task scoped out so it could ship the structural wins. Recording the real p50/p95 makes the "p95 < 500 ms @ 5 RPS" budget enforceable instead of aspirational.

  ## Done looks like
  - A reproducible k6 (or autocannon) script lives under `scripts/` and exercises GET /api/reports/:id, /api/stats, /api/stats/distribution, /api/stats/trends, /api/public/corpus-stats.
  - The captured p50/p95 numbers (per endpoint, at the documented concurrency) are committed to `docs/performance.md` under a "Measured baselines" section.
  - Any endpoint whose p95 exceeds 500 ms gets a follow-up issue with a CPU profile attached, per the task #726 acceptance criteria.

  ## Relevant files
  - `docs/performance.md`
  - `artifacts/api-server/src/routes/reports.ts`
  - `artifacts/api-server/src/routes/stats.ts`
  - `scripts/` (new k6 / autocannon harness)

title: Catch performance regressions in tests before they reach production
description:
# Add query-budget assertions for the hottest read paths

  ## What & Why
  The audit shipped DB indexes that fix today's seq scans, but there is no in-suite guard that catches a future N+1 regression. Task #726's acceptance criteria call for "a query-budget assertion in tests for the hottest query paths so a future N+1 regression fails CI" — the structural pieces (indexes, cache headers, lazy splits) are in place, but the budget tests are not.

  ## Done looks like
  - A small vitest harness wraps the pg client and counts queries fired during a test.
  - The hottest read endpoints (GET /api/reports/:id, GET /api/stats, GET /api/stats/distribution) have a query-count budget locked in. Any change that fires extra queries fails CI with a clear "expected ≤N queries, got M" diff.
  - The harness lives next to the existing in-memory db helper so future endpoints can opt in trivially.

  ## Relevant files
  - `artifacts/api-server/src/routes/__test-fixtures__/in-memory-db.ts`
  - `artifacts/api-server/src/routes/reports.ts`
  - `artifacts/api-server/src/routes/stats.ts`

title: Make the largest entry chunk smaller so the landing path loads faster
description:
# Split the 570 KB index entry chunk and surface a per-route JS budget

  ## What & Why
  The visualizer (now wired into every production build at `dist/public/stats.html`) shows the entry chunk weighing in at ~570 KB minified / ~184 KB gzip. That includes React + react-router + tanstack/query + the Layout shell and is the single biggest item the landing path downloads. Lighthouse CI catches catastrophic regressions but does not yet enforce a per-route JS budget — and the entry chunk has room to drop ~30%+ via vendor splitting (manualChunks for React/router/query) and pulling the keyboard-shortcuts modal + toaster off the critical path.

  ## Done looks like
  - `vite.config.ts` declares a `manualChunks` strategy that separates the React + router + query vendor bundle from app code.
  - A per-route JS budget (in bytes, gzip) is asserted by either an `lhci` resource-summary block or a small post-build node script that reads `stats.html`'s JSON sibling.
  - The landing-route initial JS drops by at least 25% from the recorded baseline (the target the audit task itself called out).

  ## Relevant files
  - `artifacts/vulnrap/vite.config.ts`
  - `artifacts/vulnrap/src/App.tsx`
  - `lighthouserc.json`
  - `docs/performance.md`

title: Make every clickable card keyboard-accessible (Enter/Space + focus)
description:
# Make every clickable card keyboard-accessible

  ## What & Why
  The vulnrap UI has ~12 `<div onClick={...}>` patterns (mostly card-as-button shells where the entire card is the click target). Keyboard-only and screen-reader users currently cannot activate these. The new jsx-a11y baseline disables `click-events-have-key-events` and `no-static-element-interactions` so the lint can land green; this follow-up flips them back to error and fixes each call site by either converting the wrapper to a real `<button>` or adding `role="button"` + `tabIndex={0}` + a matching `onKeyDown` handler.

  ## Done looks like
  - Both rules are re-enabled in `artifacts/vulnrap/eslint.config.js` and `pnpm exec eslint .` is clean
  - Every formerly-clicky div is operable from the keyboard (Enter / Space) with a visible focus ring
  - The "Known gaps" entry in `docs/accessibility.md` is removed

  ## Relevant files
  - `artifacts/vulnrap/src/pages/home/index.tsx` (lines 419, 535, 555)
  - `artifacts/vulnrap/src/pages/home/visitor-counter.tsx` (lines 208, 210, 212, 214, 228)
  - `artifacts/vulnrap/src/pages/home/index.tsx` line 295
  - `artifacts/vulnrap/src/pages/batch.tsx` line 199
  - `artifacts/vulnrap/src/pages/check.tsx` line 726
  - `artifacts/vulnrap/eslint.config.js` (re-enable rules)
  - `docs/accessibility.md` (drop the known-gap line)

title: Add a real captions track to the home-page rap-sheet video
description:
# Add a real captions track to the home-page rap-sheet video

  ## What & Why
  The walkthrough video on the landing page (`vulnrap-rap-sheet.mov`) currently ships without an external captions track — captions are baked into the source. We muted the video and suppressed the `jsx-a11y/media-has-caption` lint as a stop-gap, but a proper WebVTT `<track kind="captions">` is the WCAG 1.2.2 (AA) requirement and lets users who prefer text follow along without playing the audio at all.

  ## Done looks like
  - A `vulnrap-rap-sheet.vtt` file is committed alongside the video and served from the same public path
  - `<track kind="captions" srcLang="en" src="...vtt" default />` is wired into `<video>`
  - The Known-gaps entry in `docs/accessibility.md` is removed

  ## Relevant files
  - `artifacts/vulnrap/src/pages/home/video-section.tsx`
  - `artifacts/vulnrap/public/vulnrap-rap-sheet.mov`
  - `docs/accessibility.md`

title: Run automated colour-contrast checks against the live OKLCH palette
description:
# Run automated colour-contrast checks against the live OKLCH palette

  ## What & Why
  The new axe scans (both unit and Playwright) currently disable the `color-contrast` rule. axe-core in headless Chromium / happy-dom can't reliably resolve the project's OKLCH-based design tokens, which produced false positives that drowned out real findings. We need a separate pipeline that loads the resolved RGB values (e.g. via Playwright + computed styles, or a script that walks `tailwind.config.ts` + the CSS custom-properties in `index.css`) and asserts WCAG 2.1 AA contrast for every text/background pair we actually ship.

  ## Done looks like
  - A check (script or test) catches contrast regressions on the resolved palette
  - `color-contrast` is re-enabled in the Playwright and unit axe runs, or the new check replaces them
  - Failures fail CI

  ## Relevant files
  - `artifacts/vulnrap/e2e/accessibility.spec.ts`
  - `artifacts/vulnrap/src/test/axe.ts`
  - `artifacts/vulnrap/src/index.css` (token definitions)
  - `artifacts/vulnrap/tailwind.config.ts`

title: Show the running build version on the website footer
description:
# Show the running build version on the website footer

  ## What & Why
  Task #728 added `GET /api/version` so self-hosters and hosted-instance users can confirm exactly what they are talking to. The information is currently only reachable via curl. Surfacing it in the SPA footer (small "v3.8.0 · a1b2c3d" link that opens a modal with the full payload) gives non-curl users a one-glance way to confirm a deploy actually shipped, and gives bug reporters a copy-paste version string.

  ## Done looks like
  - A new tiny client hook (e.g. `useVersionInfo`) consumes `GET /api/version` via the existing generated React Query client.
  - The footer in `artifacts/vulnrap/src` renders `v<projectVersion> · <gitSha[:7]>`, links to a modal / popover showing the full payload (project, projectVersion, gitSha, specVersion, builtAt).
  - The link copies the payload to the clipboard so bug reporters can paste it into an issue.

  ## Relevant files
  - `artifacts/vulnrap/src/components/footer.tsx` (or equivalent)
  - `lib/api-client-react/src/generated` (already exposes `useGetVersion`)
  - `docs/versioning.md` (link the new UI from the "Verifying a self-hosted instance" section)

title: Block PRs that change the API contract without a changelog entry
description:
# Block PRs that change the API contract without a changelog entry

  ## What & Why
  `docs/versioning.md` and `CONTRIBUTING.md` (updated in Task #728) require every user-visible change to add an entry under `## [Unreleased]` in `CHANGELOG.md`, and require breaking changes to bump `info.version` in `lib/api-spec/openapi.yaml`. Today nothing enforces this — a PR that touches the spec or a stable route handler can land without either. A small CI check would catch the regression at PR time, not at release-cut time.

  ## Done looks like
  - A new `.github/workflows/changelog-check.yml` (or addition to `ci.yml`) runs on every PR, fails when:
    - The diff touches `lib/api-spec/openapi.yaml` or `artifacts/api-server/src/routes/` and `CHANGELOG.md`'s `## [Unreleased]` section did not gain a new bullet.
    - `scripts/openapi-breaking-diff.mjs` (Task #728) reports breaking changes against `origin/main` and `info.version` in the spec did not move.
  - Bypass via a `skip-changelog` PR label for internal-only refactors.
  - README "Public API stability" cross-links the check.

  ## Relevant files
  - `scripts/openapi-breaking-diff.mjs`
  - `.github/workflows/ci.yml`
  - `CHANGELOG.md`
  - `CONTRIBUTING.md` (the "Changelog entries" section)

title: Auto-generate the public changelog page on the website from CHANGELOG.md
description:
# Auto-generate the public changelog page on the website from CHANGELOG.md

  ## What & Why
  The repo now has a real `CHANGELOG.md` (Task #728) but the public website still has no per-release "what changed" page. The closest thing is the `/api/changelog.json` feed used by the in-app banner. Rendering `CHANGELOG.md` as a public `/changelog` page (or extending the existing feed to source from the file) gives end-users and self-hosters a single canonical place to see what shipped in each version, cross-linked from the footer's version string.

  ## Done looks like
  - A new `/changelog` page in `artifacts/vulnrap/` renders the parsed `CHANGELOG.md` (one collapsible section per release, anchor links per version).
  - Either: a tiny build-time step parses `CHANGELOG.md` into JSON for the SPA, OR the existing `/api/changelog` route is taught to read it.
  - Linked from the footer (alongside the version string from the footer follow-up) and from `README.md`.

  ## Relevant files
  - `CHANGELOG.md`
  - `artifacts/api-server/src/routes/changelog-feed.ts`
  - `artifacts/vulnrap/src` (new `changelog` route)

title: Cross-link the dry-run preview from every platform recipe
description:
# Cross-link the dry-run preview from every platform recipe

  ## What & Why
  Task #732 added `POST /api/reports/check/dry-run-batch` and a "Validate before flipping it on" section to the HackerOne recipe. The endpoint is platform-agnostic, but the other platform-specific recipes (Bugcrowd, Intigriti, GitHub Security Advisories, Jira) currently don't mention it. New recipe authors would have to rediscover the validator from scratch.

  ## Done looks like
  - Every platform-specific integration recipe under `artifacts/api-server/docs/integrations/` has a "Validate before flipping it on" section that links back to the HackerOne recipe's Step 5 (or duplicates the bash script with the platform's ingestion call swapped in).
  - The shared paragraph explicitly notes that the endpoint is platform-agnostic and the safety gates (action == AUTO_CLOSE && goldHitCount == 0) are identical across recipes.

  ## Relevant files
  - `artifacts/api-server/docs/integrations/hackerone.md` (Step 5 — already added in #732)
  - Future recipes: `bugcrowd.md`, `intigriti.md`, `ghsa.md`, `jira.md` under the same docs/integrations/ directory.

title: Show the dry-run preview in the developers UI
description:
# Show the dry-run preview in the developers UI

  ## What & Why
  The new `POST /api/reports/check/dry-run-batch` endpoint is documented in the HackerOne recipe and the OpenAPI spec, but there's no in-product surface where a triager can paste a handful of report bodies and see the preview table without writing a curl loop. A small panel on `/developers` (or a dedicated `/dry-run` page) would make the validation workflow accessible to non-CLI users and double as a marketing demo for the safety story.

  ## Done looks like
  - A web UI accepts up to 25 pasted report bodies, calls the new endpoint, and renders a sortable table with the per-item composite, action, gold-hit count, and `wouldAutoClose` flag.
  - A "Download CSV" button exports the same table for offline review by a senior triager.

  ## Relevant files
  - `artifacts/api-server/src/routes/reports.ts` (existing endpoint, lines ~1519–1670)
  - `artifacts/vulnrap/src/pages/developers/` (where the new panel/page should live)
  - `lib/api-client-react/src/generated/` (auto-generated React Query hook for the new endpoint)

title: Add automated tests for the dry-run preview endpoint
description:
# Add automated tests for the dry-run preview endpoint

  ## What & Why
  Task #732 was verified by a single live curl smoke-test against the running server. There's no automated test asserting the endpoint's contract (validation errors, oversize per-item rejection, partial per-item failure isolation, the `wouldAutoClose` gate logic). A future refactor of `performAnalysis`, the triage matrix, or the AVRI gold-hit extraction could silently break the safety gate without anyone noticing.

  ## Done looks like
  - A vitest file under `artifacts/api-server/src/routes/` covers: (a) empty/missing `reports` array → 400, (b) oversize `rawText` → 413, (c) a known-low-quality body → `wouldAutoClose=true`, (d) a body with strong technical substance → `wouldAutoClose=false` even when other items in the same batch close, (e) a per-item crash returns `error` on that item but the batch still 200s.
  - The test uses the same supertest pattern as the existing route tests in `artifacts/api-server/src/routes/*.route.test.ts`.

  ## Relevant files
  - `artifacts/api-server/src/routes/reports.ts` (the new handler, ~1519–1670)
  - `artifacts/api-server/src/routes/calibration-rename.route.test.ts` (pattern reference)

title: Show a clearer error when bots or stale tabs hit the newsletter form
description:
# Show a clearer error when bots or stale tabs hit the newsletter form

  ## What & Why
  The newsletter form now requires a proof-of-work challenge that expires after 5 minutes and can only be used once. Real users who leave the community page open in a tab for a long time will get a generic "Subscription failed — Challenge not found or expired" toast and have to guess that re-clicking will work. We should detect the expired/used-challenge case, transparently fetch a fresh challenge, and retry once before surfacing an error.

  ## Done looks like
  - When /api/newsletter/subscribe returns 403 with the challenge-expired or already-used error, the form silently fetches a new challenge, re-solves, and retries the submit once.
  - If the retry also fails, the user sees a friendly message ("Please try again") instead of the raw server error string.

  ## Relevant files
  - `artifacts/vulnrap/src/pages/community.tsx` (NewsletterForm)
  - `artifacts/api-server/src/lib/challenge.ts` (error strings to detect)

title: Move the newsletter signup challenge into a Web Worker so the page never stutters
description:
# Move the newsletter signup challenge into a Web Worker so the page never stutters

  ## What & Why
  The proof-of-work solver in NewsletterForm runs on the main thread and yields every 1024 iterations. At difficulty 4 this is fine on desktop but can briefly jank low-end mobile devices. Hoisting the solver into a Web Worker would let it run at full speed without ever blocking input or animations, and would let us raise the difficulty later without UX cost.

  ## Done looks like
  - A small reusable `pow-worker.ts` lives alongside the community page (or under `artifacts/vulnrap/src/lib/`) and accepts `{ prefix, nonce, difficulty }`, posts back the solution.
  - NewsletterForm uses the worker via `new Worker(new URL(...), { type: "module" })` so Vite bundles it correctly.
  - The "Verifying…" button state is driven by worker progress messages.

  ## Relevant files
  - `artifacts/vulnrap/src/pages/community.tsx` (current solveNewsletterChallenge)
  - `artifacts/vulnrap/vite.config.ts` (confirm worker support)

title: Add automated tests for the newsletter signup challenge so it can't silently break
description:
# Add automated tests for the newsletter signup challenge so it can't silently break

  ## What & Why
  The newsletter route now has a proof-of-work gate but no route-level test coverage. Without tests, a future refactor of the shared challenge helper or the newsletter route could quietly disable the gate and let bot signups through — exactly the regression this task was meant to prevent.

  ## Done looks like
  - A new `artifacts/api-server/src/routes/newsletter.route.test.ts` covering: GET /newsletter/challenge returns the expected shape; POST /newsletter/subscribe rejects with 400 when challenge fields are missing; rejects with 403 on a bad solution; rejects with 403 on a reused challenge; accepts a valid solution and inserts the row.
  - Tests use the same in-memory-db / mocked-DB pattern used by other `*.route.test.ts` files in this folder.

  ## Relevant files
  - `artifacts/api-server/src/routes/newsletter.ts`
  - `artifacts/api-server/src/lib/challenge.ts`
  - `artifacts/api-server/src/routes/feedback.holdout.route.test.ts` (pattern reference)

title: Show subscribers a friendly confirmation page after they click the email link
description:
# Show subscribers a friendly confirmation page after they click the email link

  ## What & Why
  Today the welcome / confirm / unsubscribe links in the newsletter email return raw JSON because the endpoints are mounted under /api. A normal subscriber clicking the link from their email client will see `{"ok":true,"removed":true,"message":"..."}` instead of a styled page that matches the rest of the site. A small static page (e.g. `/newsletter/unsubscribed`, `/newsletter/confirmed`) that calls the API behind the scenes and renders a branded result would feel much more professional and reduce confusion / support emails.

  ## Done looks like
  - Clicking the unsubscribe / confirm link in a welcome email lands on a styled page that matches the VulnRap UI, not raw JSON
  - The page surfaces the success / already-done / failure cases with the same copy variants the API returns today
  - The email link helper in `artifacts/api-server/src/routes/newsletter.ts` is updated to point at the new frontend pages, with the API endpoints kept for programmatic clients

  ## Relevant files
  - `artifacts/api-server/src/routes/newsletter.ts` (buildUnsubscribeUrl / buildConfirmUrl)
  - `artifacts/vulnrap/src/pages/` (new newsletter result pages + router wiring)
  - `artifacts/api-server/src/lib/newsletter-email.ts` (email body copy if links change)

title: Let subscribers ask for the welcome / confirm email to be re-sent
description:
# Let subscribers ask for the welcome / confirm email to be re-sent

  ## What & Why
  Right now the welcome / confirm email is sent exactly once, the moment the row is first inserted. If the email is lost in spam, the delivery webhook is offline, or the user clicked the wrong address, there's no way for them to request a fresh link — re-submitting the same address returns `alreadySubscribed: true` and silently does nothing (the original token's hash is the only thing on file, so we can't recover it). A small "resend confirmation" endpoint that mints a new token and re-mails it (with a per-address rate limit so it isn't an enumeration oracle) would close that gap.

  ## Done looks like
  - A `POST /api/newsletter/resend` endpoint accepts an email, looks up the existing row by HMAC, mints a fresh token (rotating tokenHash), and re-sends the welcome / confirm email
  - The endpoint is rate-limited per-IP and per-email so it cannot be used to flood inboxes or enumerate which addresses are subscribed
  - The community page exposes a "didn't get the email?" link next to the success message that calls the new endpoint

  ## Relevant files
  - `artifacts/api-server/src/routes/newsletter.ts`
  - `artifacts/api-server/src/lib/newsletter-email.ts`
  - `artifacts/vulnrap/src/pages/community.tsx`
  - `lib/api-spec/openapi.yaml`

title: Track welcome email delivery so silent failures don't go unnoticed
description:
# Track welcome email delivery so silent failures don't go unnoticed

  ## What & Why
  The welcome / confirm email is dispatched fire-and-forget via the delivery webhook. If the webhook is misconfigured, returning 5xx, or simply not set, subscribers silently never receive the welcome email — the only signal is a log line that nobody is watching. Persisting a small per-row delivery status (`welcome_sent_at`, `welcome_last_error`) and surfacing a count of failed deliveries on the existing reviewer-only status / calibration surface would make these silent failures visible without needing to read logs.

  ## Done looks like
  - A new column on `newsletter_subscriptions` records when the welcome / confirm email was successfully dispatched (and the last error if it failed)
  - The reviewer-only internal surface (or a dedicated row on `/api/status`) shows the count of subscribers whose welcome email is overdue / failing
  - A retry path exists either as part of the resend endpoint above or as a small background catch-up job

  ## Relevant files
  - `lib/db/src/schema/newsletter_subscriptions.ts`
  - `artifacts/api-server/src/lib/newsletter-email.ts`
  - `artifacts/api-server/src/routes/newsletter.ts`
  - `artifacts/api-server/src/routes/status.ts` or `internal.ts`

title: Make the benchmark catch shell-escape regressions for more vulnerability families
description:
# Extend shell-escape benchmark coverage to remaining AVRI families

  ## What & Why
  Task #736 added benchmark fixtures for AUTHN_AUTHZ (IDOR) and INJECTION (SQLi) where the only raw-HTTP evidence is shell-escaped `printf '...HTTP/1.1\r\n...'`. The other AVRI families (WEB_CLIENT XSS / SSRF / open redirect, DESERIALIZATION, CRYPTO, REQUEST_SMUGGLING beyond legit-03) still have no legit fixture that asserts the shell-escape unescape pipeline is load-bearing for their gold signals. A regression to `unescapeShellHttpFragments` could silently degrade those families without tripping CI.

  ## Done looks like
  - One legit fixture per remaining AVRI family lives in `artifacts/api-server/src/lib/engines/benchmark.test.ts` LEGIT_FIXTURES, with all raw-HTTP evidence inside a printf one-liner.
  - Each fixture's `expectMinScore` is pinned just above the redacted-printf score (mirroring legit-13/14 measurement comments).
  - `pnpm vitest run src/lib/engines/benchmark.test.ts` passes.

  ## Relevant files
  - `artifacts/api-server/src/lib/engines/benchmark.test.ts` (LEGIT_FIXTURES, especially legit-13/14 as the template)
  - `artifacts/api-server/src/lib/engines/avri/raw-http.ts` (`unescapeShellHttpFragments`, `augmentTextWithUnescapedHttp`)
  - `artifacts/api-server/src/lib/engines/avri/families.ts` (per-family gold signal patterns)

title: Show recent silent-replica alerts on the calibration page
description:
# Show recent silent-replica alerts on the calibration page

  ## What & Why
  The server now records every dispatched silent-replica alert in `replicaSilenceAlerts`, but reviewers can only see those alerts in the on-call channel they're paged into. Surfacing the recent history on the calibration page (alongside the existing scheduler stall log) would let reviewers correlate "Overdue" badges with the actual page that fired, and spot a flapping replica without leaving the UI.

  ## Done looks like
  - Calibration page lists the most recent silent-replica alerts (replica id, expected tick, overdue ms)
  - Data is fetched via a new lightweight read endpoint backed by `listReplicaSilenceAlerts()`
  - Empty state copy explains what triggers an alert

  ## Relevant files
  - `artifacts/api-server/src/lib/avri-drift-notifications.ts` (`listReplicaSilenceAlerts`)
  - `artifacts/vulnrap/src/pages/feedback-analytics.tsx` (calibration UI section with `isReplicaOverdue`)
  - `artifacts/api-server/src/index.ts` (new route)

title: Auto-clear stale heartbeats so retired replicas stop pinging on-call
description:
# Auto-clear stale heartbeats so retired replicas stop pinging on-call

  ## What & Why
  The new silent-replica detector pages reviewers whenever a peer heartbeat is older than its retry + grace. That's correct for a wedged replica, but a replica that was *retired* (scaled down, redeployed under a new id) leaves its last heartbeat in the shared file forever, and will keep tripping the alert on every watchdog tick (deduped per silence window, but re-firing each time the expected next tick advances). A periodic sweep that drops heartbeats older than e.g. 24h would prevent permanent paging from ghost replicas.

  ## Done looks like
  - Heartbeats older than a configurable TTL are pruned from the shared state file
  - The silent-replica detector ignores pruned entries on the next tick
  - TTL is documented and overridable via env var

  ## Relevant files
  - `artifacts/api-server/src/lib/avri-drift-notifications.ts` (`schedulerHeartbeats`, `detectAndNotifyOverdueReplicas`)

title: Make all on-disk state files crash-safe so a restart can't wipe them
description:
# Make all on-disk state files crash-safe so a restart can't wipe them

  ## What & Why
  Task #738 fixed the brute-force cooldown file so a crash mid-write
  can't leave it half-written (which the loader silently discards).
  The same single-`writeFileSync` pattern is still used by several
  other persisted state files in api-server, each of which has the
  same failure mode: a deploy / OOM / power loss during the write
  can leave a corrupt JSON blob, and the next start silently throws
  away whatever state was being tracked.

  The same write-temp-then-fsync-then-rename approach used in
  `artifacts/api-server/src/middlewares/calibration-auth-brute-force-alert.ts`
  should be extracted into a small shared helper and applied to the
  remaining persisted-state writers.

  ## Done looks like
  - A shared `atomicWriteJsonFileSync` (or similar) helper exists and is
    used everywhere persisted JSON state is written from api-server.
  - All listed call sites switch from `writeFileSync` to the helper.
  - Each call site has at least one test asserting no `*.tmp`
    siblings are left behind after a successful write.

  ## Relevant files
  - `artifacts/api-server/src/lib/avri-drift-notifications.ts` (line ~559)
  - `artifacts/api-server/src/lib/score-stability-monitor.ts` (line ~112)
  - `artifacts/api-server/src/lib/engines/avri/ai-self-disclosure.ts` (line ~276)
  - `artifacts/api-server/src/lib/engines/avri/handwavy-phrases.ts` (line ~448)
  - Reference implementation: `artifacts/api-server/src/middlewares/calibration-auth-brute-force-alert.ts` `writePersistedState`

title: Restore the reviewer dashboard's broken test suite
description:
# Restore the reviewer dashboard's broken test suite

  ## What & Why
  The internal client library that the reviewer dashboard talks to (`@workspace/api-client-react`) only re-exports its auto-generated API surface from `src/index.ts`. The hand-written helpers in `src/custom-fetch.ts` (`setCalibrationToken`, `getCalibrationToken`, `addRateLimitObserver`, `ApiError`, `RateLimitNotice`, etc.) are never re-exported from the package entry point.

  As a result, ~32 test files under `artifacts/vulnrap/src/pages` fail at module-load with `TypeError: addRateLimitObserver is not a function` / `getCalibrationToken is not a function`, and `pnpm tsc --noEmit` in `artifacts/vulnrap` reports matching `TS2305` errors. This silently disables a large chunk of the dashboard's regression coverage — including the new bulk-rearm reviewer/rationale specs added in Task #740.

  ## Done looks like
  - `pnpm vitest run` in `artifacts/vulnrap` runs every previously-passing spec without module-load errors related to the api client.
  - `pnpm tsc --noEmit` in `artifacts/vulnrap` no longer reports `TS2305` for `setCalibrationToken`, `getCalibrationToken`, `addRateLimitObserver`, or `ApiError` from `@workspace/api-client-react`.
  - The Task #740 specs in `artifacts/vulnrap/src/pages/feedback-analytics-notified-flags.test.tsx` (the two "Task #740" `it(...)` blocks) execute and pass.

  ## Relevant files
  - `lib/api-client-react/src/index.ts` (likely fix: `export * from "./custom-fetch";`)
  - `lib/api-client-react/src/custom-fetch.ts`
  - `artifacts/vulnrap/src/pages/feedback-analytics-notified-flags.test.tsx`
  - `artifacts/vulnrap/src/lib/rate-limit-cooldown.ts`
  - `artifacts/vulnrap/src/pages/feedback-analytics.tsx` (consumers of `ApiError` / `getCalibrationToken`)

title: Add a fixture exemplar for the new no-overlap fake-trace tell

# Add a fixture exemplar for the new no-overlap fake-trace tell

  ## What & Why
  Task #741 added the structural-fabrication marker `thread_role_no_overlap` that fires on fake crash traces with only 2 distinct role-tagged thread IDs (e.g. `READ ... thread T0` + `freed by thread T1` with no allocated-by line). The detector ships with unit tests but the T4_HALLUCINATED cohort in `test-fixtures.ts` only contains a 3-distinct-IDs exemplar (T4-15-fake-thread-id-mismatch, which trips the older Task #433 detector). Without a 2-IDs exemplar, the new detector is not exercised end-to-end through Engine 2 / hallucination-detector / triage scoring, and a regression that suppressed only the 2-IDs case would not be caught.

  ## Done looks like
  - A new T4_HALLUCINATED fixture exists whose ASan trace has exactly 2 distinct role-tagged thread IDs distributed across READ + freed (or freed + allocated) anchors with no overlap, plus enough secondary fabrication tells (e.g. round_function_offsets) to push the structural marker count to ≥2 and trip the hallucination override

title: Show edit and removal history for AI self-disclosure phrases
description:
# Show edit and removal history for AI self-disclosure phrases

  ## What & Why
  Reviewers can now correct typos in saved AI self-disclosure patterns (PUT) and remove them entirely (DELETE), but unlike the curated hand-wavy phrase list, there is no audit history of those changes. Hand-wavy phrases track an append-only `getHandwavyPhraseHistory()` log so reviewers can see who changed what and when. AI self-disclosure phrases only stamp `addedBy`/`addedAt` (now also `editedBy`/`editedAt`) on the live row, and an entry that gets removed leaves no trace at all. A history log would let a reviewer notice "someone deleted the prepared_using_ai default last Tuesday" or "this regex was edited 4 times in a week — maybe we should split it".

  ## Done looks like
  - Each add/edit/remove of an AI self-disclosure phrase appends an entry to a bounded history list (matching the hand-wavy phrase pattern)
  - GET /feedback/calibration/ai-self-disclosure-phrases returns the active list AND the recent history
  - A removed phrase shows up in the history with the reviewer who deleted it

  ## Relevant files
  - `artifacts/api-server/src/lib/engines/avri/ai-self-disclosure.ts` (add history accessor + persist on each mutation)
  - `artifacts/api-server/src/routes/calibration.ts` (echo history in GET/POST/PUT/DELETE responses)
  - `artifacts/api-server/src/lib/engines/avri/handwavy-phrases.ts` (reference implementation for the history log shape)

title: Persist alert acknowledgements so they survive a server restart
description:
# Persist alert acknowledgements so they survive a server restart

  ## What & Why
  Reviewer acknowledgements on calibration auth brute-force alerts currently live only in the in-memory ring buffer. Restarting the API server (or eviction of the alert from the ring buffer) loses both the alert and the ack — there is no audit trail for "we already looked at this." For an operator-facing security signal, persisting the ack alongside who/when/why would let on-call reviewers prove the alert was triaged.

  ## Done looks like
  - Acknowledgements (ip, detectedAt, ackedBy, note, ackedAt) survive an API server restart
  - Either the existing JSON state file (`statePath`) round-trips acks too, or acks are written to a small append-only log
  - The dashboard's "Acknowledged by …" badge keeps working after a restart for any alert still in the buffer

  ## Relevant files
  - `artifacts/api-server/src/middlewares/calibration-auth-brute-force-alert.ts` (state save/load surface)

title: Recognize heredoc-style HTTP smuggling reproductions
description:
# Recognize heredoc-style HTTP smuggling reproductions

  ## What & Why
  Tasks #427 and #755 added gold-signal coverage for printf '...HTTP/1.1\r\n...' / "..." and bash $'...HTTP/1.1\r\n...' shell-escaped reproductions. The other common idiom for pasting raw HTTP in a bug-bounty repro is a bash heredoc piped to nc, e.g. `nc target 80 <<'EOF' ... EOF` or `cat <<EOF | nc target 80 ... EOF`. Heredoc bodies contain literal CRLFs only when the report copies them verbatim; many reporters paste LF-only blocks expecting bash/nc to handle framing. Without explicit recognition, these legitimate reports either miss the raw_http_request / smuggled_second_request gold signals or get misclassified.

  ## Done looks like
  - The raw-HTTP detector recognises `<<['"]?EOF['"]?` ... `EOF` blocks containing an HTTP/1.x request line and feeds the body (with CRLF normalization where appropriate) through the same gold-signal / fakeness pipeline as printf and $'...' reproductions.
  - Positive + slop-revoke tests mirror the existing Task #427 / #755 pairs.

  ## Relevant files
  - `artifacts/api-server/src/lib/engines/avri/raw-http.ts` (add HEREDOC_HTTP_RE alongside SHELL_ESCAPED_HTTP_* matchers, extend unescapeShellHttpFragments / augmentTextWithUnescapedHttp).
  - `artifacts/api-server/src/lib/engines/avri/raw-http.test.ts` (mirror the Task #755 ANSI-C positive + slop tests).

title: Recognize Python-style requests.raw / b'...' HTTP reproductions
description:
# Recognize Python-style requests.raw / b'...' HTTP reproductions

  ## What & Why
  After Tasks #427 and #755, shell printf '...' / "..." / $'...' HTTP reproductions all earn gold-signal credit. The other common framing in modern bug-bounty reports is a Python snippet that builds the raw bytes literally — `payload = b"POST / HTTP/1.1\r\nHost: ...\r\n\r\n"` followed by `socket.send(payload)`, or `requests` with a custom `prepared.body = b"..."`. The byte-string literal carries the same backslash-r-backslash-n escape sequences as the shell variants but lives inside a `b"..."` / `b'...'` prefix that the current matchers don't accept.

  ## Done looks like
  - Detector recognises `b"..."` and `b'...'` Python byte-string literals containing an HTTP/1.x request line with literal \r\n escapes, unescapes them, and feeds them through the same pipeline.
  - Positive + slop-revoke tests mirror the Task #427 / #755 fixtures.

  ## Relevant files
  - `artifacts/api-server/src/lib/engines/avri/raw-http.ts` (add PYTHON_BYTES_HTTP_RE and extend unescapeShellHttpFragments / augmentTextWithUnescapedHttp).
  - `artifacts/api-server/src/lib/engines/avri/raw-http.test.ts` (positive + slop tests).

title: Add a regression test that XXE reports route to the deserialization rubric
description:
# Add a regression test that XXE reports route to the deserialization rubric

  ## What & Why

The fix is currently only covered indirectly via existing soft-citation/slop-signals tests — there is no explicit assertion that classifyReport returns family DESERIALIZATION for an XXE report (with or without a cited CWE-611). A targeted test would catch any future regression where someone re-adds 611 to WEB_CLIENT.memberCwes or reorders the FAMILIES array.

  ## Done looks like
  - A new test asserts `classifyReport("...XXE...", [])` and `classifyReport("...XXE...", ["CWE-611"])` both return family.id === "DESERIALIZATION".
  - A test asserts the xxe_doctype_entity (18pt) and xxe_entity_load_flag (12pt) gold signals fire end-to-end on a representative XXE report.

title: Recognise vulnerability shorthand in Arabic, Turkish, Polish, and Vietnamese reports
description:
# Recognise vulnerability shorthand in Arabic, Turkish, Polish, and Vietnamese reports

  ## What & Why
  Tasks #424 and #754 covered the top nine non-English researcher languages
  (ES/PT/FR/RU/JA + DE/ZH/IT/KO). The next tranche by report-corpus volume
  is Arabic, Turkish, Polish, and Vietnamese — researchers writing in
  those languages still drop into the underspecified bucket whenever they
  omit the English acronym. Adding their long-form translations to
  `VULN_TYPE_PATTERNS` keeps closing the gap.

  ## Done looks like
  - Each of the 16 entries in `VULN_TYPE_PATTERNS` recognises the most
    common long-form phrasing in AR / TR / PL / VI for that class.
  - Soft-citation tests cover at least one phrasing per added language so a
    regression in any single language's regex surfaces in CI.
  - Arabic patterns handle RTL text without the /i flag (case is
    meaningless in Arabic); Polish and Turkish use /i so diacritics
    (ą/ć/ę/ł/ń/ó/ś/ź/ż, ç/ğ/ı/ö/ş/ü) and Vietnamese uses /i so the
    combining-diacritic Latin letters case-fold correctly.

  ## Relevant files
  - `artifacts/api-server/src/lib/engines/extractors.ts` (VULN_TYPE_PATTERNS)
  - `artifacts/api-server/src/lib/engines/soft-citation.test.ts` (test block)

title: Track which vulnerability-shorthand patterns actually fire on real reports
description:
# Track which vulnerability-shorthand patterns actually fire on real reports

  ## What & Why
  `VULN_TYPE_PATTERNS` now contains hundreds of regexes across ten
  languages and sixteen vulnerability classes. We have no instrumentation
  showing which language patterns earn their keep on the real report
  corpus vs which ones never match anything outside the unit test that
  ships them. Without that telemetry we can't tell when a translation is
  wrong (e.g. a typo or an unidiomatic phrasing nobody actually uses)
  until a researcher complains.

  ## Done looks like
  - A one-shot script in `artifacts/api-server/scripts/` (or a vitest
    bench-style runner) walks the existing report corpus, runs
    `detectVulnerabilityType` against each report, and produces a CSV /
    markdown table of (language, vulnerability class, hit count).
  - The output makes it obvious which (language, class) pairs are
    carrying weight and which are dead weight, so future translation
    PRs can be data-driven instead of guess-driven.

  ## Relevant files
  - `artifacts/api-server/src/lib/engines/extractors.ts`
  - `artifacts/api-server/test/regression/golden-corpus.json`
  - `artifacts/api-server/scripts/` (new script)

title: Add a startup smoke test that fails CI if the api-server bundle exits before listening
description:
# Add a startup smoke test that fails CI if the api-server bundle exits before listening

  ## What & Why
  Task #758 fixed a regression where the bundled `backfill-vulnrap.ts` CLI side effect ran at api-server startup and `process.exit(0)`'d before `app.listen` bound a port. The fix moves the CLI to its own entry (`backfill-vulnrap-cli.ts`), but there's no dedicated guard against the same class of bug recurring (e.g. another module sneaking in a top-level `process.exit`, a top-level `await` that throws, or a future esbuild bundling change).

  A focused smoke test that builds the bundle and spawns `dist/index.mjs` with a real `PORT`, asserting that the process logs `Server listening` (and stays alive long enough to be killed) would catch this whole family of regressions in CI in well under 10 seconds.

  ## Done looks like
  - A vitest test (e.g. `src/server-startup.smoke.test.ts`) builds the api-server bundle, spawns `dist/index.mjs` with a free port + `NODE_ENV=""`, and asserts the readiness log appears before any exit

title: Add an automated check that the API server starts cleanly without firing maintenance scripts
description:
# Add an automated check that the API server starts cleanly without firing maintenance scripts

  ## What & Why
  Task #760 fixed a bug where the legacy report rescore backfill silently kicked off on every API server boot because the CLI runner was bundled into `dist/index.mjs`. The fix was architectural (split the CLI into its own module), but there is no regression test that asserts the api-server bundle never auto-runs maintenance jobs. A short test that boots `dist/index.mjs` and asserts no `[backfill]` log line appears (and no maintenance `process.exit` fires before the server is ready) would catch any future module that accidentally re-introduces a top-level side effect into the bundle (e.g. seed, backfill-avri-family, backfill-fabricated-evidence).

  ## Done looks like
  - A vitest e2e test boots the built `dist/index.mjs` and confirms no maintenance-script logs appear during startup
  - The test fails if any of the backfill modules grows a new auto-run path

  ## Relevant files
  - `artifacts/api-server/src/backfill-vulnrap-cli.ts`
  - `artifacts/api-server/src/backfill-vulnrap.ts`
  - `artifacts/api-server/src/backfill-avri-family.ts`
  - `artifacts/api-server/src/backfill-fabricated-evidence.ts`
  - `artifacts/api-server/src/seed.ts`

title: Move the seed and other backfill scripts to the same library + CLI split as backfill-vulnrap
description:
# Move the seed and other backfill scripts to the same library + CLI split as backfill-vulnrap

  ## What & Why
  `backfill-vulnrap.ts` was the only maintenance script that exported a function imported by the api-server, so it was the only one that could misfire. But `seed.ts`, `backfill-avri-family.ts`, and `backfill-fabricated-evidence.ts` still mix library code and a top-level CLI runner in the same file. If any of them is ever imported from the server bundle they will recreate the exact same bug Task #760 fixed. Apply the same pattern preventatively: keep the implementation as a pure library, move the argv parsing + `process.exit` runner into a sibling `*-cli.ts` file, and update `build.mjs` + the matching `pnpm` scripts.

  ## Done looks like
  - Each maintenance script has a thin `*-cli.ts` entry that owns argv parsing and `process.exit`
  - The corresponding implementation files have no top-level side effects
  - `build.mjs` entry points and `package.json` scripts point at the new `*-cli.mjs` bundles

  ## Relevant files
  - `artifacts/api-server/src/seed.ts`
  - `artifacts/api-server/src/backfill-avri-family.ts`
  - `artifacts/api-server/src/backfill-fabricated-evidence.ts`
  - `artifacts/api-server/build.mjs`
  - `artifacts/api-server/package.json`

title: Fix the auth-status probe so it works when no reviewer token is configured
description:
# Fix the auth-status probe so it works when no reviewer token is configured

  ## What & Why
  Two tests in `artifacts/api-server/src/routes/calibration-auth.route.test.ts`
  ("calibration auth-status probe (CALIBRATION_TOKEN unset / open mode)")
  fail on main today, independently of Task #761. They expect the
  `/feedback/calibration/auth-status` probe to report
  `mutationsAllowed=true` when the server has no reviewer token configured
  (open / single-reviewer / local-dev fallback per Task #117), so the dashboard
  can render a neutral "not required" chip instead of a misleading
  "missing token" warning. The handler currently returns
  `mutationsAllowed=false` in that mode, so the dashboard mis-renders the
  auth chip in any deployment that runs without CALIBRATION_TOKEN.

  ## Done looks like
  - GET /feedback/calibration/auth-status with no token configured returns
    `mutationsAllowed=true` whether or not the caller sends a token header.
  - Both currently-failing tests in calibration-auth.route.test.ts pass.

  ## Relevant files
  - `artifacts/api-server/src/routes/calibration.ts` (auth-status handler)
  - `artifacts/api-server/src/routes/calibration-auth.route.test.ts` (lines 717–767, the open-mode describe block)

title: Refuse to start in production when CORS is wide open
description:
# Refuse to start in production when CORS is wide open

  ## What & Why
  Task #766 added a startup warning when ALLOWED_ORIGINS is unset, but the API still boots and accepts every cross-origin request. In a production deployment that is almost always a misconfiguration — operators should be forced to set the allow-list explicitly (or opt in to open mode with a separate flag) before the server starts serving traffic.

  ## Done looks like
  - When NODE_ENV=production and ALLOWED_ORIGINS resolves to "unset", the API exits non-zero at startup with a clear error pointing operators at the env var.
  - An explicit opt-in (e.g. ALLOW_ANY_ORIGIN=true) lets operators keep the open-mode behaviour when they really want it, and that opt-in is logged at warn level.
  - Existing dev/test workflows continue to start with no extra configuration.

  ## Relevant files
  - `artifacts/api-server/src/lib/allowed-origins.ts`
  - `artifacts/api-server/src/app.ts`

title: Surface the triage recommendation as a typed field on the Go SDK
description:
# Surface the triage recommendation as a typed field on the Go SDK

  ## What & Why
  The HackerOne recipe's Go example (Task #770) has to reach into `CheckResult.Raw["recommendation"]` and re-unmarshal it to read `recommendation.action` / `recommendation.reason`, because the SDK's `CheckResult` (and `ReportAnalysis`) don't expose a typed `Recommendation` field. The Python SDK has the same gap. Promoting the field to a first-class typed shape would simplify every PSIRT integration that uses the SDK and would let us drop the `summarize` helper from the recipe snippets.

  ## Done looks like
  - `CheckResult` and `ReportAnalysis` in `sdks/go/vulnrap/types.go` expose a typed `Recommendation` (with `Action`, `Reason`, and `ChallengeQuestions`) that is populated from the server payload.
  - The Python SDK's equivalent dataclass exposes the same field.
  - The HackerOne and (forthcoming) Bugcrowd Go snippets are simplified to read the recommendation directly off the typed field.
  - Tests cover both the present and absent cases.

  ## Relevant files

title: Extend punctuation-separator support to the POSTSLOT regex
description:
# Extend punctuation-separator support to the POSTSLOT regex

  ## What & Why
  Task #386 relaxed the INLINE regex separator class to `[s,(–—]+`.
  Task #742 mirrored that for DQUOTE and SQBRACKET. The POSTSLOT regex
  (`PROSE_PAYLOAD_PLACEHOLDER_POSTSLOT_RE`, around line 611 of
  `artifacts/api-server/src/lib/engines/avri/raw-http.ts`) currently uses
  `[s,–—]+` — it already has comma and dashes but is missing the
  opening-paren `(` that the other three regexes all accept. Slop authors
  can currently sidestep the POSTSLOT form by writing
  ```<inject>`` (payload) we sent` with a paren separator.

  ## Done looks like
  - POSTSLOT separator class updated from `[s,–—]+` to `[s,(–—]+`
  - Mirror tests added in `raw-http.test.ts` (paren-separator positive case + neutral-identifier negative case)
  - All existing tests still pass

  ## Relevant files
  - `artifacts/api-server/src/lib/engines/avri/raw-http.ts` (~line 611)
  - `artifacts/api-server/src/lib/engines/avri/raw-http.test.ts` (~line 1447–1536)

title: Make the cohort baseline percentile exact instead of bucket-approximated
description:
# Make the cohort baseline percentile exact instead of bucket-approximated

  ## What & Why
  The /api/cohort/baseline endpoint returns a 10-bucket histogram and the UI computes a percentile from those bins (mid-rank within the score's bucket). That keeps the payload tiny but means percentile values quantise in 10-point steps and a report scoring 61 vs 69 can show the same "Higher than X%" label. For a ribbon meant to give "instant context" this is fine, but a small server-side query that returns the exact percentile of the queried score (e.g. `percent_rank()` over the cohort) would tighten the label without bloating the response.

  ## Done looks like
  - /api/cohort/baseline accepts an optional `score` query parameter and returns an exact `percentile` field alongside the histogram.
  - The UI prefers the server's exact percentile when present and falls back to the bucket math otherwise.
  - Tests pin the exact percentile against a small fixture cohort.

  ## Relevant files
  - `artifacts/api-server/src/routes/cohort.ts`
  - `artifacts/vulnrap/src/components/cohort-baseline-ribbon.tsx`
  - `lib/api-spec/openapi.yaml` (`CohortBaseline` schema)

title: Backfill engine version pins on legacy reports
description:
# Backfill engine version pins on legacy reports

  ## What & Why
  Task #624 added the new `engine_versions` JSONB column on `reports` and populates it for newly scored reports, but every report scored before the migration shipped has `engine_versions = NULL`. The diagnostics-panel footer hides for those rows, and any future score-evolution timeline / drift dashboard that joins on engine version will silently exclude them.

  A one-shot backfill should walk legacy rows and stamp them with the engine versions inferred from `vulnrap_engine_results` / `created_at` (best-effort: pin to the oldest known semver, e.g. `{linguistic, substance, cwe, avri, fusion} = "3.0.0"` for anything pre-Sprint-12, with a more specific pin where the blob makes it derivable).

  ## Done looks like
  - A script (mirroring the existing `rescore-backfill-scheduler` pattern) walks reports where `engine_versions IS NULL` in batches and writes a best-effort version pin.
  - Idempotent: re-running the script does not overwrite already-populated rows.
  - The diagnostics panel footer renders for backfilled legacy reports.

  ## Relevant files
  - `artifacts/api-server/src/lib/engine-versions.ts`
  - `artifacts/api-server/src/lib/rescore-backfill-scheduler.ts` (pattern reference)
  - `lib/db/src/schema/reports.ts`

# Cache the public drift summary so the transparency page loads instantly

  ## What & Why
  `GET /api/public/drift-summary` re-runs the full internal AVRI drift compute (16-week DB scan + per-row family resolution) on every request. Since the underlying weekly aggregate only changes a few times a day at most, the public endpoint should return a short-TTL cached payload so a burst of `/transparency` visits doesn't repeatedly hammer the reports table.

  ## Done looks like
  - The public drift summary is cached server-side with a short TTL (e.g. 5 minutes) and a manual invalidation hook.
  - A `Cache-Control` header is set so CDNs / browsers can also cache the response.
  - A test confirms a second request inside the TTL does not re-run `generateAvriDriftReport`.

title: Stop the rescore log from growing forever
description:
# Stop the rescore log from growing forever

  ## What & Why
  The nightly score-stability job appends one row per report per day to `report_rescore_log` with no retention policy. At current report volume that table will grow unbounded and eventually slow the daily summary query. The chart only ever looks back 7 days (capped at 90 in code), so anything older than ~90 days is dead weight.

  ## Done looks like
  - Rows older than the chart's max lookback are pruned automatically (either as part of the nightly tick or via a separate startup-migration-style job).
  - The retention window is configurable via env var, with a safe default.

  ## Relevant files
  - `artifacts/api-server/src/lib/score-stability-monitor.ts`
  - `artifacts/api-server/src/lib/score-stability-scheduler.ts`
  - `lib/db/src/schema/report_rescore_log.ts`

  ## Done looks like

title: Capture the actual scoring engine version on every analysis

# Capture the actual scoring engine version on every analysis

  ## What & Why
  The new score evolution timeline (task #621) wants to show "this scored 62 in v3.10, 58 in v3.11" but the system has no first-class version field — the timeline currently derives a coarse label ("AVRI" / "Composite v3" / "Legacy" / "Reconstructed") from feature flags on the analysis trace. Persisting an explicit semver-style scoring engine version on each `analysis_traces` row (and on the rescore audit entries) would let the timeline show real version numbers and make calibration drift investigations much sharper.

  ## Done looks like
  - Each new analysis trace records the running scoring engine version (e.g. read from package.json or a dedicated constant).
  - Rescore audit entries on `vulnrap_engine_results.rescoreHistory` also carry the version that produced the new score.
  - The score-history endpoint surfaces the real version string instead of the feature-flag-derived label, and the results-page timeline displays it.

title: Persist per-engine sub-scores in analysis traces
description:
# Persist per-engine sub-scores in analysis traces

  ## What & Why
  The score evolution timeline can show full per-engine sub-scores for the *current* score (read from `vulnrap_engine_results.engines`) but past entries only get the engine list with the composite score as a fallback, because `analysis_traces.trace` stores `enginesUsed` and the composite but not each engine's numeric score / verdict / confidence. Recording those values on every trace would let reviewers hover any historical point and see real engine-by-engine numbers, not a placeholder.

  ## Done looks like

A small offline calibration pass would let us tune the weights so the preview number matches the real score within ±10 points on the corpus.

title: Block deploys when the scoring gate fails (not just warn)
description:
# Block deploys when the scoring gate fails

  ## What & Why
  The pre-merge scoring gate (Task #641) currently runs in `scripts/post-merge.sh` and only **warns** on failure — it cannot actually block, because the merge has already happened by the time post-merge runs. As a result a tier-flipping change can slip through to production unless someone reads the post-merge log. We need a real enforcement point that runs *before* the deploy build, exits non-zero, and fails the deploy.

  ## Done looks like
  - The scoring gate (or a slimmed-down version of it) runs as part of the deploy build command in `.replit` (currently `pnpm --filter @workspace/vulnrap run build && pnpm --filter @workspace/api-server run build && pnpm store prune`).
  - A failing gate stops the deploy with a clear error pointing at the per-fixture diff and the `SCORING_GATE_BYPASS=1` escape hatch.
  - The deploy-time gate is documented alongside the post-merge gate in the README "Pre-merge scoring gate" section.

  ## Out of scope
  - Replacing the post-merge gate (it stays as the early-warning layer).
  - Running the full 100-report replay if the deploy environment cannot reach the production DB — fall back to the golden-corpus-only check in that case.

  ## Relevant files
  - `scripts/scoring-gate.sh`
  - `scripts/post-merge.sh`
  - `.replit` (deploy build/run lines)
  - `README.md` ("Pre-merge scoring gate" section)

title: Track scoring-gate flip rate over time so drift is visible
description:
# Track scoring-gate flip rate over time

  ## What & Why
  The pre-merge scoring gate (Task #641) computes a tier-flip rate every run but throws the number away after printing it. We have no visibility into whether the rate is creeping up over weeks of small changes (death-by-a-thousand-cuts calibration drift) or holding steady. Persisting each run's flip rate + per-fixture diff gives reviewers a trend line and lets us spot the slow leak that the per-run 0.5% threshold cannot.

  ## Done looks like
  - Each `scripts/scoring-gate.sh` run appends a JSON record (`{timestamp, commit, totalReports, flipCount, flipRate, topDiffs}`) to a stored history (DB table or append-only file).
  - The calibration page (or a small new admin page) renders the last ~30 runs as a sparkline plus a table of the most recent per-fixture diffs.
  - README documents how to inspect the trend and how to clear/reset the history after an intentional re-baselining.

  ## Out of scope
  - Alerting / paging when the trend slope crosses a threshold (could be a follow-up to *this* follow-up).

  ## Relevant files
  - `scripts/scoring-gate-replay.mjs`
  - `artifacts/api-server/test/regression/scoring-replay.test.ts`
  - `artifacts/api-server/src/routes/calibration.ts`
  - `lib/db/src/schema/` (likely a new `scoring_gate_runs` table)

title: Strip injection text before sending reports to the LLM scorer
description:
# Strip injection text before sending reports to the LLM scorer

## What & Why
Task #642 added a `prompt_injection_attempted` signal that detects when a report tries to manipulate the LLM scorer. The signal is currently observation-only — the raw text (including the injection) is still passed verbatim to the LLM substance pass. Use the new signal to redact / neutralize the injection sentences before the LLM call so the scorer cannot be coerced even when a future jailbreak slips past upstream defences.

## Done looks like
- When `promptInjectionAttempted` is true, the matched spans are wrapped/replaced (e.g. with `[REDACTED-INJECTION]`) in the text passed to `analyzeSlopWithLLM`.
- A test asserts the LLM input no longer contains the original injection phrasing for each fixture in `prompt-injection.test.ts`.
- The unredacted text is still used for the heuristic / engine pipeline so content-quality scoring is unchanged.

## Relevant files
- `artifacts/api-server/src/routes/reports.ts` (LLM call site around line 342)
- `artifacts/api-server/src/lib/linguistic-analysis.ts` (`promptInjectionMatches`)

Equivalent slop reports in Spanish, German, Japanese, Russian, Portuguese and Arabic are scored as "Clean" because none of those phrases match. The new `per_language_agreement_rate` metric in the calibration report will surface this disparity, but the underlying detection gap remains.

  ## Done looks like
  - Linguistic analyzer recognizes equivalent AI-cliché phrases in at least the 6 languages covered by the test battery
  - Translated slop fixtures in `multilingual-fixtures.ts` start producing slop scores comparable to their English originals (without weakening the English fixture)
  - `per_language_agreement_rate` reaches >= 80% with the original-strength English slop fixture restored

Reviewers who notice a new "tell" for an agent (e.g. a fresh ChatGPT phrasing, a new Cursor agent narration pattern) can't add it without a code change + redeploy. title: Validate AI-agent fingerprint accuracy against a real-world labelled corpus
description:
# Real-world accuracy benchmark for the agent-fingerprint detector

  ## What & Why
  Task #644 ships with 8 hand-crafted fixture pairs that confirm the detector wires up correctly, but they're synthetic. We don't yet know how well the heuristic holds up on real reports authored by each agent. A benchmark run would let us tune weights and add/remove rules with data instead of intuition.

  ## Done looks like
  - A labelled corpus of ≥10 real reports per agent class (gpt4 / claude / gemini / cursor-agent / replit-agent / human) lives under `artifacts/api-server/test/fixtures/agent-fingerprint-corpus/`.
  - A test (or script) runs the detector across the corpus and reports a per-class precision/recall table; the test fails if overall accuracy regresses below an agreed baseline.
  - Documented confusion patterns (e.g. "Cursor often misclassifies as GPT-4") feed back into rule tuning.

title: Alert when holdout accuracy drops below in-sample accuracy
description:
# Alert when holdout accuracy drops below in-sample accuracy

  ## What & Why
  Task #640 added a holdout evaluation endpoint that reports honest precision/recall on a 20% partition that calibration never sees. Today nothing watches the gap — if calibration starts overfitting (in-sample metrics improve while holdout metrics decay) the team will only notice if someone manually opens /feedback-analytics. A scheduled check that flags a meaningful gap (e.g. holdout F1 trails in-sample F1 by more than 0.1, or holdout accuracy drops below a floor) would surface drift automatically.

  ## Done looks like
  - A scheduler job evaluates the holdout vs in-sample gap on a daily cadence
  - When the gap exceeds a configurable threshold, an entry is written to the existing drift / notification system
  - The threshold and cadence are documented and configurable

  ## Relevant files
  - `artifacts/api-server/src/routes/feedback.ts` (GET /feedback/holdout-eval — reuse the same math)
  - `artifacts/api-server/src/lib/avri-drift-notifications.ts` (existing scheduler/notification pattern)
  - `artifacts/api-server/src/index.ts` (scheduler wiring)

title: Backfill holdout flag for any future schema migrations that recreate IDs
description:
# Make sure holdout assignment survives ID-changing migrations

  ## What & Why
  The holdout flag is derived deterministically from the feedback row id (`abs(hashtext(id::text)) % 5 = 0`). If a future migration ever rewrites or reassigns user_feedback ids (e.g. switching from serial to UUID, or compacting rows), every row's holdout assignment will silently change and historical holdout metrics will become meaningless. We should either (a) freeze isHoldout at insert time only and never recompute, or (b) document a clear rule that any ID-rewriting migration must also re-stamp isHoldout from the OLD id mapping.

  ## Done looks like
  - A short note in the user_feedback schema file and startup-migrations file warning future maintainers
  - Or, a stronger guarantee: a constraint / test that the holdout fraction stays close to 20% over time

  ## Relevant files
  - `lib/db/src/schema/user_feedback.ts`
  - `artifacts/api-server/src/lib/startup-migrations.ts` (the 2026-05-03-add-user-feedback-holdout block)
  - `artifacts/api-server/src/routes/feedback.ts` (POST /feedback stamping logic)

title: Alert reviewers when shadow scoring drifts too far from live
description:
# Alert reviewers when shadow scoring drifts too far from live

  ## What & Why
  Task #639 added a reviewer-only `/feedback-analytics` panel and a `GET /api/internal/shadow-drift` endpoint that lists divergent reports, but the divergence is *passive* — reviewers only see it if they happen to open the page. To make Shadow Scoring Mode trustworthy as a pre-rollout signal, we need an active alert (e.g. dispatch via the existing `AVRI_DRIFT_WEBHOOK_URL` channel that score-stability uses) when the divergent-rows ratio or the legit↔slop tier-flip count crosses a configurable threshold within the lookback window.

  ## Done looks like
  - A nightly (or every-N-minutes) sweep evaluates shadow vs live divergence on `report_shadow_scores`
  - A configurable threshold (env-tunable) triggers a deduplicated webhook alert with link to the reviewer panel
  - A unit test covers threshold + dedup behaviour analogous to `score-stability-monitor.test.ts`

  ## Relevant files
  - `artifacts/api-server/src/lib/scoring-shadow.ts` (current shadow runner)
  - `artifacts/api-server/src/lib/score-stability-monitor.ts` (alert pattern to mirror)

# Mirror /reports/check submissions through shadow scoring too

  ## What & Why
  Task #639 explicitly scoped shadow scoring to the `POST /reports` (persisted) flow only and left `POST /reports/check` (instant-check) untouched. Instant-check is the higher-volume surface and the one users see live, so its scoring rule changes are the riskiest to roll out blind. Extending shadow scoring to instant-check (without persisting per-row to keep volume down — e.g. sample 1-in-N or aggregate-only counters) would close the biggest blind spot in the rollout signal.

  ## Done looks like
  - Instant-check requests optionally invoke the shadow pipeline behind a sample-rate env knob
  - Aggregate divergence counters surface alongside the persisted-report drift table
  - Toggle/sample-rate behaviour has unit-test coverage

  ## Relevant files
  - `artifacts/api-server/src/routes/reports.ts` (instant-check handler — fire-and-forget hook site)

Adding an in-memory-DB route test (mirroring `reports.score-history.test.ts`) plus a Playwright pass over the panel would catch regressions the unit tests can't.

  ## Done looks like
  - Route test against the in-memory DB harness covers 401, 200-with-no-rows, 200-with-divergent-rows

A small "+12 vs default" delta next to the live score would make the educational value much clearer — users can see not just where the score landed, but how much each lever moved it.

title: Add automated tests for the scoring playground math
description:
# Add automated tests for the scoring playground math

  ## What & Why
  The new `/playground` page implements its own client-side scoring math (per-engine signal averages combined by weighted mean, with engines that have all signals toggled off excluded from the denominator). This logic isn't covered by tests, so regressions to the formula or the "all signals off" edge case would silently mislead users.

  ## Done looks like
  - A test file alongside the page renders the playground and asserts:
    - Default score for each baked-in example matches a known value.
    - Toggling all signals in one engine off excludes that engine from the final score (no NaN, no divide-by-zero).
    - Moving a weight slider to 0 makes that engine contribute nothing.
    - "Reset to defaults" restores the original toggles and weights.
  - Tests follow the same Vitest + Testing Library pattern as the other `artifacts/vulnrap/src/pages/*.test.tsx` files.

  ## Relevant files

# Let users customize their own keyboard shortcuts

  ## What & Why
  The current shortcut set is fixed. Heavy users often have muscle memory from other tools and would benefit from being able to remap keys (or disable specific shortcuts that conflict with their browser/OS). This was explicitly listed as out of scope for the initial shortcuts task and is the natural next step.

  ## Done looks like

  - Custom bindings persist in localStorage and override the defaults.
  - The reference modal reflects the user's current bindings.

title: Show the embed badge directly on each report's results page
description: # Show the embed badge directly on each report's results page ## What & Why Task #664 added the public `GET /api/embed/badge.svg?id=VR-XXXX` endpoint and a snippet block on the developers page. Submitters who land on a report's results page still don't see a one-click way to grab the embed code for *their* report. Surfacing the badge + copyable HTML/Markdown snippets on the results page (where the report code is already in scope) closes the loop and is the natural way most users will discover the feature. ## Done looks like - A small "Embed this badge" block on the report results page renders a live `` of the badge for the current report id. - Copy-paste HTML and Markdown snippets prefilled with the actual VR-XXXX code are available next to the preview. - Hidden / non-public reports either suppress the block or render the same graceful "unknown" badge. ## Relevant files - `artifacts/vulnrap/src/pages/results.tsx` - `artifacts/vulnrap/src/pages/api.tsx` (existing snippet block — keep both in sync) - `artifacts/api-server/src/routes/embed.ts` (endpoint contract)

# Let badge embedders pick light or dark themes

  ## What & Why
  Current badge styles use a single colour palette designed for light README backgrounds. The "social" style in particular looks washed out on dark GitHub themes. Add a `theme=light|dark` query param and an "auto" option that emits CSS `prefers-color-scheme` swatches inside the SVG so a single embed adapts to both.

  ## Done looks like
  - /api/embed/badge.svg accepts `theme=light|dark|auto` (default light to preserve current behavior)
  - All five styles render with theme-appropriate label/value backgrounds and text colors
  - /badges gallery shows a theme toggle and previews each style in both modes
  - Unit tests cover the new theme branches in renderBadgeSvg

title: OSS community scaffolding (CoC, SECURITY, CHANGELOG, templates, CODEOWNERS)
description:
# OSS Community Scaffolding

## What & Why
The repo has a license, README, CONTRIBUTING, NOTICE, and threat model, but is missing the standard files and folders that GitHub and the wider open-source community look for first when they land on a project. Adding them lowers the bar for new contributors, sets expectations for behaviour and security disclosure, and makes the project look maintained at a glance — all without changing any product code.

## Done looks like
- A `.github/` directory exists with issue templates (bug, feature, security-redirect), a pull-request template, and a `CODEOWNERS` file pointing at the appropriate maintainers.
- `CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md` (Contributor Covenant 2.1 or equivalent) is in the repo root.
- `SECURITY.md` is in the repo root (today the README links to a GitHub security policy that does not exist as a file in the repo) and matches the disclosure path already advertised in `/.well-known/security.txt`.
- `CHANGELOG.md` is in the repo root with an initial "Keep a Changelog"-style entry that summarises the current state and notes the existing version.
- A `GOVERNANCE.md` (or equivalent section appended to CONTRIBUTING) describes who can merge, how releases are cut, and how decisions are made between REMEDiS / COMPLiTT and outside contributors.
- README links to all the new files from a single "Community" section.
- A short "Funding" pointer (GitHub `FUNDING.yml` or a README note) is added if the maintainers want one — leave commented-out if not.

## Out of scope
- CI workflow files (covered by the GitHub Actions task).
- Dependabot / CodeQL config (covered by the GitHub Actions task).
- ESLint, formatting, or pre-commit hook configuration (separate task).
- Any product/code changes.

## Steps
1. **Issue & PR templates** — Add bug, feature-request, and "I think I found a vulnerability" (redirect-only) issue templates under `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/`, plus a PR template that asks for linked issue, summary, screenshots if UI, and a checklist for typecheck/tests/codegen.
2. **Code of Conduct** — Add the Contributor Covenant 2.1 with the project's actual contact address filled in.
3. **In-repo SECURITY.md** — Move the security disclosure policy into the repo so the README link resolves and so `github.com/.../security/policy` renders. Mirror the email and response-time commitments already in `/.well-known/security.txt`.
4. **CHANGELOG.md** — Add a `Keep a Changelog` style file. Seed it with one "Unreleased" section and one entry capturing the current released version (bullet-point what already shipped at a high level, drawn from README "Composite Scoring" + recent merged tasks).
5. **CODEOWNERS** — Map `lib/db/`, `lib/api-spec/`, `artifacts/api-server/`, `artifacts/vulnrap/`, `docs/`, and `.github/` to the appropriate maintainer GitHub handles. Confirm the handles with the user before writing them in.
6. **Governance note** — Add a short `GOVERNANCE.md` (or section in CONTRIBUTING) covering: who can merge, release cadence, how to propose a breaking change, how to request a new maintainer.
7. **README wiring** — Add a "Community" section linking CoC, SECURITY, CHANGELOG, GOVERNANCE, CONTRIBUTING from one place.

## Relevant files
- `README.md`
- `CONTRIBUTING.md`
- `NOTICE.md`
- `LICENSE`
- `threat_model.md`
- `artifacts/vulnrap/public`
