title: Make the Go SDK fail loudly when the OpenAPI spec drifts
description:
# Make the Go SDK fail loudly when the OpenAPI spec drifts

  ## What & Why
  The new Go SDK at `sdks/go/vulnrap/` hand-mirrors a subset of the OpenAPI shapes (`ReportAnalysis`, `CheckResult`, `PlatformStats`, `ScoreBreakdown`, etc.). If `lib/api-spec/openapi.yaml` drops or renames a required field, the SDK will silently keep returning zero-value Go fields and tests will still pass — bad for downstream Go users. A drift check would catch this in CI.

  ## Done looks like
  - A new check (script under `sdks/go/vulnrap/scripts/` or a Node script invoked from CI) parses `lib/api-spec/openapi.yaml` and asserts that every `required` field on the four schemas the Go SDK exposes (`ReportAnalysis`, `CheckResult`, `PlatformStats`, `ScoreBreakdown`) maps to a typed Go struct field with the right JSON tag.
  - The check fails with an actionable message when fields drift (added/removed/renamed/retyped).
  - Wired into the existing repo-level lint/test pipeline so it runs alongside the Go test.

  ## Relevant files
  - `lib/api-spec/openapi.yaml`
  - `sdks/go/vulnrap/types.go`
  - `sdks/go/vulnrap/client_test.go`

title: Parallelization runbook for the active task queue
description:
# Parallelization runbook for the active task queue

## What & Why
We have 210 active tasks (100 Drafts, 100 Active, 10 in flight) and a small handful of hot files that many of them all want to edit (e.g. `feedback-analytics.tsx` at 18k lines, `reports.ts` at 2.7k, `calibration.ts` at 2.3k, `diagnostics-panel.tsx` at 2.2k). Without an explicit parallelization plan, large batches will collide on these hot files, drift each other, and serialize behind merge conflicts — exactly the failure mode we keep hitting.

This task produces a single regeneratable runbook that:
1. Inventories every active task,
2. Extracts each task's declared `## Relevant files` block,
3. Builds the file → tasks conflict graph,
4. Groups tasks into ordered "waves" where every task in a wave is file-disjoint from every other task in the same wave,
5. Identifies hot files that force serialization and lists the longest hot-file queues so we can decide whether to split them.

The runbook is the single source of truth a human (or the next planning session) consults to decide which batch to release next, instead of guessing.

## Done looks like
- A new doc at `artifacts/api-server/docs/parallelization-runbook.md` that contains:
  - Headline counts (total active, in-flight, by surface bucket).
  - "Hot files" table: file path, lines-of-code, count of active tasks claiming it, ref list.
  - Wave plan: numbered waves where every task in a wave is file-disjoint from every other task in the same wave, ordered to drain hot-file queues last.
  - Per-wave: task ref, title, files-touched count, total LOC of touched files (rough conflict-blast-radius proxy).
  - "Cannot parallelize" callouts: tasks whose `Relevant files` block is missing, vague, or claims everything (these need replanning before they can be batched).
  - A "Recommended next batch" section that names the top wave that is safe to release right now given the current 10 in-flight tasks.
- A regenerator script at `scripts/regenerate-parallelization-runbook.mjs` (no external deps, ESM) that calls `listProjectTasks`-equivalent data via the same project-task surface used by other scripts in `scripts/`, parses each task's plan body, and emits the doc.
- A `--check` mode that exits non-zero when regenerating would change the doc (ignoring the `Generated:` header line), matching the convention from `scripts/regenerate-state-of-platform.mjs`.
- A sibling sanity test `scripts/regenerate-parallelization-runbook.test.mjs` that asserts: idempotent regen, hot files list non-empty, every active task ref appears exactly once, no task appears in two waves, "Cannot parallelize" section enumerates tasks with empty `Relevant files`.
- A short pointer added to `artifacts/api-server/docs/README.md` so future agents discover the runbook.

## Out of scope
- Actually launching any tasks. The runbook is read-only guidance.
- Re-planning tasks whose `Relevant files` block is vague — they get listed in the "Cannot parallelize" section but are not edited here.
- Touching task descriptions, dependencies, or state.
- Any code changes outside `scripts/` and `artifacts/api-server/docs/`.

## Steps
1. **Data ingestion** — Pull every PROPOSED / PENDING / IN_PROGRESS / BLOCKED_BY_DRIFT task with descriptions. Parse the markdown for the `## Relevant files` section; normalize each entry to a file path (strip `:line-range` suffixes for the conflict graph but preserve them in the doc).
2. **Conflict graph** — Build `file → [taskRef, taskRef, ...]`. Hot file = file with >= 3 active tasks claiming it. Conflict edge = two tasks share at least one file.
3. **Wave assignment** — Greedy graph-coloring on the conflict graph: walk tasks in priority order (in-flight first to schedule around them, then by surface bucket, then by ref) and assign each to the lowest-numbered wave with no conflict. Cap waves at the surface team's stated concurrency limit so we don't over-promise.
4. **Hot-file callouts** — For each hot file, list its task queue length and a one-line note recommending whether to (a) keep the queue serial, (b) split the file, or (c) route through a single owning task.
5. **Doc emit + regen script + check mode + test** — Mirror the conventions already established by `scripts/regenerate-state-of-platform.mjs` and `scripts/regenerate-state-of-platform.test.mjs` (no external deps, `--check` ignores `Generated:` line, sibling `*.test.mjs` runs the parser-drift checks).
6. **README pointer** — Add a one-line link from `artifacts/api-server/docs/README.md` to the new runbook, with the regen + check commands.

## Relevant files
- `scripts/regenerate-state-of-platform.mjs`
- `scripts/regenerate-state-of-platform.test.mjs`
- `artifacts/api-server/docs/README.md`
- `.local/tasks/`

title: Codebase provenance, wiring, and lifecycle headers (lib/*)
description:
# Codebase provenance, wiring, and lifecycle headers

## What & Why
A spot audit shows the codebase has good per-edit provenance (131 of 174 source files cite a `Task #N`) but is weak on the three documentation axes that matter most when the queue churn is this high:

- **Wiring**: only 23 files explain what routes / UI surfaces / scheduled jobs they're consumed by. New contributors (and future agents) have to grep to discover whether a `lib/` helper is dead, internal, or wired to a public endpoint.
- **Lifecycle**: only 1 file in the entire codebase has a `@deprecated` marker. We have known soft-deprecated paths (e.g. duplicate gold-signal counter on `AvriEngine2Result`, JSONB feed-row fallback after the backfill) with no machine-readable signal of "kill when X is true."
- **Top-of-file headers**: 25+ long-lived `lib/*` modules (`logger.ts`, `redactor.ts`, `triage-assistant.ts`, `calibration.ts`, `active-verification.ts`, `factual-verification.ts`, `linguistic-analysis.ts`, `evidence-quality.ts`, `human-indicators.ts`, `internal-consistency.ts`, `repro-recipe.ts`, `triage-recommendation.ts`, `cache-service.ts`, `claim-specificity.ts`, `sloppiness.ts`, `spectral-analysis.ts`, `section-parser.ts`, `similarity.ts`, `challenge.ts`, `scoring-config.ts`, `pdf.ts`, `sanitize.ts`, `public-url.ts`, `startup-migrations.ts`, `config-notices.ts`) have no header at all — you have to read 100 lines of code to find out what each one is for.

This task establishes a standard, applies it to the safe (non-hot) files, and adds a linter so the standard sticks.

## Done looks like
- A short convention doc at `artifacts/api-server/docs/file-header-convention.md` defining the canonical top-of-file header block:

  ```
  /**
   * <module name> — <one-line purpose>
   *
   * Wired to:
   *   - <route or UI surface or job> (file:line or task ref)
   *   - ...
   *
   * Why: <design intent in 1–3 sentences>
   *
   * Lifecycle: ACTIVE | SOFT_DEPRECATED | CANDIDATE_FOR_REMOVAL
   *   <If not ACTIVE: "Kill when:" condition, e.g. "consumers stop reading
   *    legacy_jsonb fallback (Task #551)" or "all reports re-scored after
   *    Task #389 backfill completes in production">
   *
   * Origin: Task #<N> (and links to follow-up tasks if they reshape this).
   */
  ```

- The header applied to every `artifacts/api-server/src/lib/*.ts` file in the list above that:
  - Has no current top-of-file header,
  - Is NOT a hot file (defined as: appears in the `## Relevant files` of any IN_PROGRESS or PENDING task — read the project-task descriptions at apply time and skip those files to avoid merge conflicts),
  - Is NOT a `*.test.ts` file or generated code under `lib/api-client-react/src/generated/` or `lib/api-zod/src/generated/`.
- A linter at `scripts/check-file-headers.mjs` (no external deps, ESM, `--check` mode) that:
  - Reads `artifacts/api-server/src/lib/**/*.ts` (excluding tests + generated),
  - Asserts each file's first non-shebang line begins a `/**` block containing the literal tokens `Wired to:`, `Why:`, `Lifecycle:`, and `Origin:`,
  - Asserts `Lifecycle:` value is one of the three enum values,
  - Skips files explicitly marked with the leading comment `// header-exempt: <reason>` so reviewers can opt out trivially mechanical files.
- A sibling sanity test `scripts/check-file-headers.test.mjs` that runs the linter against a fixture directory of good/bad sample files (in `scripts/__fixtures__/header-linter/`) and asserts pass/fail outcomes.
- The convention doc linked from `artifacts/api-server/docs/README.md` and from `replit.md` so future contributors find it.
- For the 3 known soft-deprecated paths called out above, the corresponding files get `Lifecycle: SOFT_DEPRECATED` with a concrete `Kill when:` condition referencing the task that will sunset them. (The duplicate gold-signal counter is in `lib/engines/engines.ts` or wherever `AvriEngine2Result` is exported — verify at apply time.)

## Out of scope
- Hot files (`feedback-analytics.tsx`, `reports.ts`, `calibration.ts`, `diagnostics-panel.tsx`, `routes/reports.ts`, anything > 2k lines) — they will get headers in a follow-up task once their in-flight queue has drained, to avoid merge conflicts with active reviewers.
- Test files. The convention applies to production source only.
- Generated files under `lib/api-client-react/src/generated/` and `lib/api-zod/src/generated/`.
- Mass-renaming or refactoring code; this task is purely additive comments.
- Frontend `vulnrap/src/` files — they are higher-churn and out of scope for this first pass; a separate task can extend the linter to that tree once the lib/ pass is stable.
- Adding `@deprecated` JSDoc tags to individual exports (the file-level `Lifecycle:` marker is the contract for now).

## Steps
1. **Convention doc** — Write `artifacts/api-server/docs/file-header-convention.md` with the template above, the rationale, the three-value `Lifecycle:` enum + when to use each, the `// header-exempt:` opt-out, and one fully-worked example each for an ACTIVE and a SOFT_DEPRECATED file. Link it from the docs README and `replit.md`.
2. **Linter + fixture tests** — Implement `scripts/check-file-headers.mjs` and `scripts/check-file-headers.test.mjs` mirroring the conventions of `scripts/regenerate-state-of-platform.mjs` and its sibling test. Linter must support `--check` and print a useful "missing token X in file Y" message.
3. **Hot-file exclusion list** — Before applying headers, query the active project tasks (PENDING + IN_PROGRESS + BLOCKED_BY_DRIFT), parse each `## Relevant files` block, and build the set of files to SKIP. Print the skipped list at the top of the apply pass so reviewers can see what was deferred.
4. **Apply headers** — Add the header block to every eligible `artifacts/api-server/src/lib/*.ts` file. For each file: (a) one-line purpose line, (b) wiring inferred from `rg -n "<exportName>" artifacts/api-server/src` to find call sites, (c) origin task by `git log -S` or by reading the most recent provenance comment in the file, (d) lifecycle = ACTIVE unless the file is on the known soft-deprecated list.
5. **Run linter on apply pass** — `node scripts/check-file-headers.mjs --check` exits 0 over the touched set; fails informatively over excluded hot files (so the follow-up task knows what's left).
6. **CI hookup** — Add a one-line note to `replit.md` and the convention doc that the linter should be run as a pre-merge check; do NOT actually wire it into a workflow — that is a separate ops task.

## Relevant files
- `artifacts/api-server/src/lib/logger.ts`

title: 24-hour retrospective: what we shipped on the free credits
description:
# 24-hour retrospective: what we shipped on the free credits

## What & Why
The free-credit burn-down has driven a huge wave of merges over the last day. We need a single, honest, publishable retrospective that tells the story to (a) the founder for the morning review, (b) the public-facing audience on the marketing site, and (c) the team as an engineering changelog. The goal is one document that pulls real numbers from git + the project-task ledger + the scoring engine's own metrics, not vibes.

## Done looks like
- A new doc at `artifacts/api-server/docs/retrospectives/<YYYY-MM-DD>-24h-recap.md` with these sections, in this order:
  1. **Headline** — single-sentence summary of the 24-hour window, plus 4–6 bold stat chips (tasks merged, files changed, lines added/removed, new pages live, new integrations live, engine signals added).
  2. **What landed** — grouped by surface bucket (engine/scoring, content/integration, UI, security, calibration/reviewer, platform/ops, test infra). Each bucket: 1-paragraph plain-English summary + bullet list of merged task refs with one-line outcomes.
  3. **How the analysis got better** — measured side-by-side. For every scoring signal we touched in the window (gold signals, fabricated-evidence flags, handwavy-phrase impact, crash-trace, hallucination cohort, linguistic, sloppiness, claim-specificity, evidence-quality, internal-consistency, AVRI raw-http), report:
      - Before/after on the scoring regression corpus (`pnpm --filter @workspace/api-server run test -- --run real-world-corpus`-style),
      - Delta in mean composite score on the legit/slop benchmark fixtures,
      - Any new fixtures added to the corpus (count + categories).
     Fall back to "no measurable change" honestly when there isn't a number.

- Running the scoring regression corpus live from the script (slow + flaky); the script summarizes the most recent on-disk run only.

- Publishing externally (Twitter, Discord, blog) — the doc is the artifact; distribution is the founder's call.
- Any speculative metrics. If a number isn't measurable from the repo or the scoring outputs, say so.
- Touching the parallelization runbook (Task #927) or the header convention doc (Task #928) — this task only consumes their outputs if available, never modifies them.

## Steps
1. **Window pick** — Default to "since 24h before the script's invocation timestamp"; allow `--since=<ISO>`. Print the window at the top of the doc.
2. **Commit/task ingestion** — `git log --since` for merge commits, parse `Task #N` from messages, hydrate task title + surface bucket from the project-task ledger. Bucket rules mirror the parallelization runbook (Task #927) — if that runbook ships first, import its bucketing helper rather than duplicating it.
3. **Scoring delta extraction** — Look for the most recent scoring-regression / real-world-corpus output under `artifacts/api-server/test-output/` (or wherever the test runner already writes it; verify at apply time). For each signal touched in window, diff the corpus run against the run immediately preceding the window. Cap at "no measurable change" when the on-disk artifact doesn't exist or the touched signal isn't covered by the corpus.
4. **URL inventory** — Walk `artifacts/vulnrap/src/App.tsx` route table at HEAD vs. at the window's start commit; emit added routes.
5. **Doc emit + check mode + test** — Mirror `scripts/regenerate-state-of-platform.mjs` conventions exactly.
6. **README + footer pointer** — Append a one-line link to `artifacts/api-server/docs/README.md`. Add a tiny "Latest 24h recap →" link to `artifacts/vulnrap/src/components/layout.tsx` footer (NOT to changelog.tsx, NOT to architecture.tsx) — single-line addition, low conflict risk.
7. **Honesty pass** — Re-read the generated doc and replace any superlative we can't back with a number ("massively improved" → "+X% on Y fixture set" or remove).
